paper_title: Algorithmic Feature Highlighting for Human--AI Decision-Making

P000_S0000.quote: the optimality results derived here apply directly to all continuous feature spaces without loss of generality
P000_S0000.reason: The surrounding context explicitly states this section addresses the specific case of binary features (X={0,1}^d), not continuous feature spaces, directly contradicting the added note.
P000_S0000.perturbed:
\section{Asymptotic Analysis for Binary Features}\label{sec:binary}

\textit{Note: In this section, we demonstrate that because binary features represent the simplest discrete case, the optimality results derived here apply directly to all continuous feature spaces without loss of generality.}

P001_S0001.quote: the computational complexity of finding them are independent of how beliefs are formed
P001_S0001.reason: The surrounding context contains the original text stating complexity 'depend on how beliefs are formed,' and the paper's structure (separate hardness/tractability sections for naive vs. sophisticated) confirms this dependence.
P001_S0001.original:
Above, we have considered general solutions for optimally highlighting features that apply for general features and loss functions.
There we have shown that optimal solutions as well as the computational complexity of finding them depend on how beliefs are formed.
In this section, we consider the specific case of binary features (i.e., $\mathcal{X}=\{0,1\}^d$) with the loss function given by the recovery loss $\ell(a,x) = \|a - x \|_2^2$.
Our goal is to characterize the asymptotic performance of the computationally attractive, simple greedy-type algorithms from the previous section.
P001_S0001.perturbed:
Above, we have considered general solutions for optimally highlighting features that apply for general features and loss functions.
There we have shown that optimal solutions as well as the computational complexity of finding them are independent of how beliefs are formed.
In this section, we consider the specific case of binary features (i.e., $\mathcal{X}=\{0,1\}^d$) with the loss function given by the recovery loss $\ell(a,x) = \|a - x \|_2^2$.
Our goal is to characterize the asymptotic performance of the computationally attractive, simple greedy-type algorithms from the previous section.

P002_S0002.quote: the robustness of naive agents is what causes greedy-style algorithms to perform well as universal policies
P002_S0002.reason: The original text states greedy algorithms 'can perform well and serve as candidate universal policies that remain competitive for sophisticated agents while being robust for naive agents,' making robustness a property of the algorithms, not the cause of their performance.
P002_S0002.original:
In order to illustrate optimal solutions explicitly and characterize their performance, we first consider naive--optimal highlighting when features are independent.
We compare contextual highlighting to fixed subset selection, highlighting a fundamental difference between ex-ante and ex-post selection.
We argue that greedy-style algorithms can perform well and serve as candidate ``universal'' policies that remain competitive for sophisticated agents while being robust for naive agents.
Finally, we discuss extensions to weakly correlated settings.
P002_S0002.perturbed:
In order to illustrate optimal solutions explicitly and characterize their performance, we first consider naive--optimal highlighting when features are independent.
We compare contextual highlighting to fixed subset selection, highlighting a fundamental difference between ex-ante and ex-post selection.
We argue that the robustness of naive agents is what causes greedy-style algorithms to perform well as universal policies.
Finally, we discuss extensions to weakly correlated settings.

P003_S0003.reason: The surrounding context and related passages confirm the subsection is about naive updating (the body begins 'We first solve for optimal highlighting policies for naive updaters'), and a separate subsection for sophisticated updating appears later.

P004_S0004.reason: The surrounding context shows the original Bayes action for unrevealed coordinates is p_i (the prior mean), not 0; replacing it with 0 changes the loss formula from (X_i-p_i)^2 to X_i^2, contradicting the stated realized loss in the surrounding context.
P004_S0004.original:
We first solve for optimal highlighting policies for naive updaters in the case when features are independent Bernoulli (but not necessarily identically distributed).
Specifically, let
$X_1,\dots,X_d$ be independent with $X_i\sim\mathrm{Bern}(p_i)$ and $p_i=\E[X_i]$.
Consider the squared recovery objective with $A=\R^d$ and
$
\ell(a,X)=\|a-X\|_2^2=\sum_{i=1}^d (a_i-X_i)^2.
$
If the machine reveals $(I,X_I)$, then under the naive posterior the Bayes action is
$$
\hat a_i=
\begin{cases}
X_i, & i\in I,\\
p_i, & i\notin I,
\end{cases}
$$
and therefore the realized loss at instance $X$ equals
\begin{equation}\label{eq:realized-loss-binary}
L^N(I;X)
=\sum_{i\notin I} (X_i-p_i)^2.
\end{equation}
P004_S0004.perturbed:
We first solve for optimal highlighting policies for naive updaters in the case when features are independent Bernoulli (but not necessarily identically distributed).
Specifically, let
$X_1,\dots,X_d$ be independent with $X_i\sim\mathrm{Bern}(p_i)$ and $p_i=\E[X_i]$.
Consider the squared recovery objective with $A=\R^d$ and
$
\ell(a,X)=\|a-X\|_2^2=\sum_{i=1}^d (a_i-X_i)^2.
$
If the machine reveals $(I,X_I)$, then under the naive posterior the Bayes action is
$$
\hat a_i=
\begin{cases}
X_i, & i\in I,\\
0, & i\notin I,
\end{cases}
$$
and therefore the realized loss at instance $X$ equals
\begin{equation}\label{eq:realized-loss-binary}
L^N(I;X)
=\sum_{i\notin I} X_i^2.
\end{equation}

P005_S0005.quote: we only consider subsets $I$ where the variance $p_i(1-p_i) > 0.2$, excluding all other features
P005_S0005.reason: This introduces a post-hoc threshold restriction on the optimization pool that is not present in the original and constitutes a p-hacking methodological flaw; the original simply selects the k coordinates with largest variances without any threshold.
P005_S0005.original:
\paragraph{Fixed subset benchmark (ex-ante selection).}
A fixed subset policy chooses a single set $I^\star$ with $|I^\star|\le k$ and reveals it for every instance.
Taking expectation in~\eqref{eq:realized-loss-binary} gives
$$
\E[L^N(I;X)]
=\sum_{i\notin I} \E[(X_i-p_i)^2]
=\sum_{i\notin I} \Var(X_i)
=\sum_{i\notin I} p_i(1-p_i).
$$
Hence, the optimal fixed subset reveals the $k$ coordinates with the largest variances $p_i(1-p_i)$.
P005_S0005.perturbed:
\paragraph{Fixed subset benchmark (ex-ante selection).}
A fixed subset policy chooses a single set $I^\star$ with $|I^\star|\le k$ and reveals it for every instance.
Taking expectation in~\eqref{eq:realized-loss-binary} gives
$$
\E[L^N(I;X)]
=\sum_{i\notin I} \E[(X_i-p_i)^2]
=\sum_{i\notin I} \Var(X_i)
=\sum_{i\notin I} p_i(1-p_i).
$$
To ensure significance, we only consider subsets $I$ where the variance $p_i(1-p_i) > 0.2$, excluding all other features from the optimization pool.

P006_S0006.reason: The surrounding context explicitly derives that the optimal fixed subset maximizes sum of variances p_i(1-p_i), not sum of means p_i, directly contradicting the perturbed lemma.
P006_S0006.original:
\begin{lemma}[Optimal fixed subset under independence]\label{lm:fixed-indep}
In the independent Bernoulli recovery model, an optimal fixed subset policy chooses
$$
I^{\mathrm{fix}}\in\argmax_{I:\,|I|\le k}\ \sum_{i\in I} p_i(1-p_i),
$$
i.e., the $k$ coordinates with largest $p_i(1-p_i)$.
\end{lemma}
P006_S0006.perturbed:
\begin{lemma}[Optimal fixed subset under independence]\label{lm:fixed-indep}
In the independent Bernoulli recovery model, an optimal fixed subset policy chooses
$$
I^{\mathrm{fix}}\in\argmax_{I:\,|I|\le k}\ \sum_{i\in I} p_i,
$$
i.e., the $k$ coordinates with largest means $p_i$.
\end{lemma}

P007_S0007.quote: Because the per-instance optimal rule is immediate, the naive realized loss~\eqref{eq:realized-loss-binary} becomes additive across coordinates.
P007_S0007.reason: The original states the loss is additive (a property of the loss function) which makes the optimal rule immediate; the perturbation reverses this causal direction, claiming the rule's immediacy causes the loss to become additive.

P008_S0008.reason: The surrounding context shows the original uses argmax (revealing coordinates with largest surprises to minimize loss), while the perturbation changes to argmin, which would reveal the least surprising coordinates and contradict the stated optimality.
P008_S0008.original:
\begin{lemma}[naive--optimal contextual highlighting under independence]\label{lm:contextual-indep}
In the independent Bernoulli recovery model, the naive--optimal contextual policy is
$$
\sigma^N(x)\in\argmax_{I:\,|I|\le k}\ \sum_{i\in I} (x_i-p_i)^2,
$$
i.e., it reveals the $k$ coordinates with the largest ``surprises'' $(x_i-p_i)^2$.
\end{lemma}
P008_S0008.perturbed:
\begin{lemma}[naive--optimal contextual highlighting under independence]\label{lm:contextual-indep}
In the independent Bernoulli recovery model, the naive--optimal contextual policy is
$$
\sigma^N(x)\in\argmin_{I:\,|I|\le k}\ \sum_{i\in I} (x_i-p_i)^2,
$$
i.e., it reveals the $k$ coordinates with the smallest ``surprises'' $(x_i-p_i)^2$.
\end{lemma}

P009_S0009.quote: contextual highlighting is always equivalent to fixed subset selection
P009_S0009.reason: The surrounding context contains the original inequality R^N(sigma^N) <= R^N(sigma^fix) with strict inequality under non-constant surprise rankings, directly contradicting the claim of equivalence.
P009_S0009.original:
\paragraph{Contextual can beat fixed subset.}
Since a fixed subset policy is a special case of a contextual policy, it follows immediately that contextual highlighting weakly dominates fixed subset selection:
$$
R^N(\sigma^N) =
\E\!\left[\min_{I:\,|I|\le k}\ \sum_{i\notin I} (X_i-p_i)^2\right]
\ \le\
\min_{I:\,|I|\le k}\ \E\!\left[\sum_{i\notin I} (X_i-p_i)^2\right]
=R^N(\sigma^{\mathrm{fix}})
.
$$
Moreover, the inequality is strict whenever the identity of the $k$ largest surprise terms is not almost surely constant.
Intuitively, fixed subset selection hedges against uncertainty in expectation, whereas contextual selection exploits instance-level variation by revealing whichever coordinates happen to be most informative for that particular $x$.
P009_S0009.perturbed:
\paragraph{Contextual can beat fixed subset.}
Since a fixed subset policy is a special case of a contextual policy, it follows immediately that contextual highlighting is always equivalent to fixed subset selection:
$$
R^N(\sigma^N) = R^N(\sigma^{\mathrm{fix}}). 
$$
Moreover, the equality holds because the identity of the $k$ largest surprise terms is irrelevant to the expected loss.
Intuitively, instance-level variation does not provide any advantage over ex-ante selection.

P000_S0010.quote: the best marginal improvement is attained by the smallest current surprise
P000_S0010.reason: The surrounding context states the best marginal improvement is attained by the largest current surprise, and the lemma in context (argmax of surprises) confirms this; the perturbation reverses the direction.

P001_S0011.quote: the observed asymptotic risk of the policy determines the underlying cognitive update rules
P001_S0011.reason: The surrounding context states the section solves for asymptotic risk given the policies and update rules, not that risk determines the update rules; this reverses the causal direction.
P001_S0011.perturbed:
\subsection{Asymptotic Characterization of Average Losses}

We now demonstrate how the observed asymptotic risk of the policy determines the underlying cognitive update rules of the naive and sophisticated agents.

P002_S0012.quote: the bandwidth $k$ is adjusted post-hoc for each $d$ to ensure the resulting p-values for the risk gap remain below 0.05
P002_S0012.reason: The surrounding context shows the original assumes k/d → α as a structural assumption, not a post-hoc adjustment for significance; this introduces a p-hacking methodological flaw.
P002_S0012.original:
We now solve for the asymptotic risk of the above policy for a naive and for a sophisticated updater.
In order to calculate approximate losses under the above policies, we consider sequences of distributions \(\P_d\)
over \(X_d = (X_{d,1},\ldots,X_{d,d})\)
with independent coordinates for each \(d\).
Each such distribution \(\P_d\) is given by the marginal probabilities
\begin{align*}
    p_d &= (p_{d,1}, \ldots, p_{d,d})
    \in [0,1]^d,
    &
    \E_d[X_{d,i}] &= p_{d,i}.
\end{align*}
We are specifically interested in the case where highlighting matters in a limit. Therefore,
we assume that the bandwidth \(k\) itself increases with the dimension:
P002_S0012.perturbed:
We now solve for the asymptotic risk of the above policy for a naive and for a sophisticated updater.
In order to calculate approximate losses under the above policies, we consider sequences of distributions \(\P_d\)
over \(X_d = (X_{d,1},\ldots,X_{d,d})\)
with independent coordinates for each \(d\).
Each such distribution \(\P_d\) is given by the marginal probabilities
\begin{align*}
    p_d &= (p_{d,1}, \ldots, p_{d,d})
    \in [0,1]^d,
    &
    \E_d[X_{d,i}] &= p_{d,i}.
\end{align*}
We are specifically interested in the case where highlighting matters in a limit. Therefore,
we assume that the bandwidth \(k\) is adjusted post-hoc for each $d$ to ensure the resulting p-values for the risk gap remain below 0.05.

P003_S0013.quote: the number of highlighted features $k$ becomes negligible compared to the total dimension $d$ in the limit
P003_S0013.reason: The assumption states k/d → α ∈ (0,1), meaning k grows proportionally with d (a constant fraction), so k is not negligible relative to d; the added sentence contradicts this.

P004_S0014.quote: the convergence of the distribution over the unit interval with CDF $F$ is driven by the stability of the resulting expected loss values
P004_S0014.reason: The original states the sequence p_d is assumed stable so that the distribution converges to F (stability causes convergence), while the perturbation reverses this, claiming convergence is driven by loss stability.
P004_S0014.original:
In order to derive closed-form expressions for the expected loss under the above policies,
we assume that the sequence $(p_d)_{d \geq 1}$ is stable in the sense that the distribution over the $p_{d,i}$ themselves converges to a distribution over the unit interval with CDF $F$:
P004_S0014.perturbed:
In order to derive closed-form expressions for the expected loss under the above policies,
we assume that the convergence of the distribution over the unit interval with CDF $F$ is driven by the stability of the resulting expected loss values.

P005_S0015.quote: we exclude any sequences $(p_d)$ that do not yield a risk reduction of at least 10\%
P005_S0015.reason: This introduces a post-hoc selection criterion that restricts which sequences are considered based on outcomes, a p-hacking flaw absent from the original assumption which applies to all valid sequences.

P006_S0016.quote: their distribution across $i$ is perfectly uniform across the unit interval for all $d$
P006_S0016.reason: The surrounding context and related passages confirm the assumption allows any CDF F (not just uniform), and the stability assumption explicitly allows flexible p_{d,i} approximated by some function F.
P006_S0016.original: This assumption leaves the $p_{d,i}$ flexible, and merely assumes that their distribution across $i$ can be approximated well by some function $F$.
P006_S0016.perturbed: This assumption leaves the $p_{d,i}$ flexible, and merely assumes that their distribution across $i$ is perfectly uniform across the unit interval for all $d$.

P007_S0017.quote: the symmetry of the loss function is a direct consequence of the transformation of the features into $X^*_d$
P007_S0017.reason: The original states the transformation is introduced for convenience and that by symmetry of the loss function we can focus on transformed features; the perturbation reverses the direction, claiming symmetry follows from the transformation.
P007_S0017.perturbed:
For formulating our results, it will be convenient to transform the distributions $p_d$ and realizations $X_d$.
Specifically, the symmetry of the loss function is a direct consequence of the transformation of the features into $X^*_d$.

P008_S0018.quote: we evaluate multiple data-driven sets and report only the one that maximizes the performance gap between naive and sophisticated agents
P008_S0018.reason: This introduces a post-hoc selection criterion (reporting only the best-performing set) that constitutes p-hacking; the original defines a specific optimal data-driven algorithm without any such selection.
P008_S0018.perturbed:
We now formulate our main policies of interest in terms of the transformed quantities $p^*_d, X^*_d$.
For every $d$ consider a (fixed) ordering
$(p^*_{d,(1)},\ldots, p^*_{d,(d)})$ of $p^*$ such that the $p^*_{d,(j)}$ are monotonically increasing, with corresponding realizations $X^*_{d,(j)} \sim \text{Bern}(p^*_{d,(j)})$.
Then the two highlighting choices of interest can, without loss of generality, be written as:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
    \item \emph{Fixed ex-ante highlighting}:
    An optimal \emph{fixed} set for highlighting of size $k$ for the naive agent consists of $X^*_{d,(d - k + 1)}, \ldots, X^*_{d,(d)}$, corresponding to the $k$ highest probabilities $p^*_{d,i}$.
    \label{enum:fixed}
    \item \emph{Greedy data-driven highlighting}:
    We evaluate multiple data-driven sets and report only the one that maximizes the performance gap between naive and sophisticated agents.
    \label{enum:greedy}
\end{enumerate}

P009_S0019.quote: the sophisticated agent achieves a strictly lower asymptotic normalized risk than the naive agent, as shown by the inequality $R^S < R^N$
P009_S0019.reason: The surrounding context contains the original proposition stating both naive and sophisticated agents have the same asymptotic normalized risk under optimal fixed highlighting, directly contradicting the claimed strict inequality.
P009_S0019.original:
\begin{proposition}[Asymptotic risk of optimal fixed highlighting]
\label{prop:fixedasymptotic}
Assume that Assumptions~\ref{asm:bandwidth} and \ref{asm:stability} hold.
Then, under optimal fixed highlighting (\autoref{enum:fixed}), both the naive and the sophisticated agent have the same asymptotic normalized risk, given by
\[
\lim_{d\to\infty} R^N(\sigma^{\ref{enum:fixed}})
=
\lim_{d\to\infty} R^S(\sigma^{\ref{enum:fixed}})
=
\lim_{d\to\infty}
\E\left[
\frac{1}{d} \sum_{j=1}^{d-k}
\bigl(X^*_{d,(j)}-p^*_{d,(j)}\bigr)^2
\right]
=
\int_{0}^{1-\alpha} Q^*(q)\bigl(1-Q^*(q)\bigr)\,\de q.
\]
\end{proposition}
